candidates.title: The pointed-arch tablet is the universal mark of inscribed law. The .precept file IS this tablet: self-contained, complete, and immutable. The three bright rules on its face are the precepts themselves — not scattered logic, not runtime guards, but laws carved into the definition that hold forever.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The law tablet means a Precept definition is a self-contained contract because its rules are inscribed once and then hold structurally.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted for the clearest law-and-contract metaphor with a strong silhouette.

candidates.title: Amber rules on near-black stone: what is inscribed in warm gold cannot be in error; what is not inscribed does not exist. The warm palette communicates authority and permanence rather than cold precision — the .precept file as ancient immutable law, not a technical document.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The amber tablet means Precept rules are durable law because only what is inscribed in the definition is allowed to exist.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted as a warmer variant of the law-tablet family with strong permanence cues.

candidates.title: A dimensionally exact isometric cube: the compiled Precept definition as a material object. One block, one definition, completely self-contained. The same solid gives the same measurements from any angle — like Precept's determinism: same definition plus same data always equals the same outcome.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The isometric block means determinism because one compiled contract stays self-consistent from every angle and yields the same result each time.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted for making determinism and one-definition completeness feel material and exact.

candidates.title: The P exists only as absence — a window cut through solid amber material. The surrounding field is the unbreakable governed contract; the P-shaped void proves that inside every Precept domain, everything is always visible and inspectable by design.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The negative-space P means inspectability because the governed contract is the solid field and the interior is visible only through what the definition allows.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted for combining ownable letterform recognition with a strong inspectability metaphor.

candidates.title: A heavy bold P where the bowl counter is a perfect square rather than the standard D-curve. The round outer bowl represents organic business complexity; the square counter shows compile-time precision imposed inside it. The square also sharply differentiates from Prefect's standard rounded P mark.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The square-counter P means precision inside complexity because the contract imposes exact structure within messy domain shapes.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted for a strong letterform with a specific precision cue that differentiates it from generic rounded P marks.

candidates.title: A solid gold tile with a dark ruled void cut entirely through it, edge to edge. The gold field is the definition: complete, bounded, immovable. The ruled void is the precept itself — it cannot be filled because it runs the full depth of the material. You cannot undo a rule that is structural.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The ruled tile means Precept's law is carved into a fixed medium because the governing void cuts through the entire definition, making the rule impossible to close or override.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted as a radical departure that passes the rubric threshold; bold gold palette and carved-rule metaphor are completely new to the set.

candidates.title: The keystone locks an arch by geometry alone: remove it and collapse is instant, but with it in place the structure cannot fail. Precept's rules make invalid states impossible by the shape of the definition itself — not runtime guards, but structural impossibility.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The keystone means structural impossibility because the whole system only holds when the governing rule-shape is in place.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted for a strong architectural metaphor that explains structural validity without diagramming workflows.

candidates.title: A solid ring represents the complete, self-contained Precept definition; three precisely cut channels at 120° are the only structural paths through it — CreateInstance, Fire, Inspect. Invalid states have no opening, so they cannot exist.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The governed ring means valid operations are structural openings because anything not cut into the contract simply has no path.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted for clearly encoding governed entry points inside a single self-contained form.

candidates.title: The compass defines the governed radius before any line is drawn — constraint precedes action. Precept works the same way: the .precept definition sets every valid boundary before runtime begins. The pivot is the definition; the arc is the only valid circumference; the arms show where the rules reach.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The compass means Precept defines exact limits before execution because the tool sets the governed radius before anything can be drawn outside it.
candidates.decision: Above threshold; teal palette and V-plus-arc silhouette are genuinely new to the set, but conceptual bridge is one step removed from Precept's prevention guarantee.

candidates.title: The letter P stands for the word 'precept' — a binding rule — and the extreme typographic weight communicates immovability. The single horizontal groove at the bowl connection is a carved rule, suggesting laws inscribed into material rather than enforced at runtime.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The bold P means immovable rule because the letterform reads like carved material rather than a flexible symbol.
candidates.decision: Shortlisted as the simplest direct letterform with clear weight and authority.

candidates.title: In mathematics, an invariant is a property that does not change under any transformation. Precept's rules are invariants: no event, no data value, no code path can make the definition untrue. The circle is the governed domain; the bisecting horizontal rule is the invariant — it holds at the same position regardless of what exists inside.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The invariant line means Precept's rules hold unconditionally because the bisecting rule maintains its exact position regardless of any state change within the governed domain.
candidates.decision: Kept for a strong mathematical precision metaphor; silhouette is slightly generic (circle + line) but clean and distinctly different from the current set.

candidates.title: The plumb bob finds true vertical by gravity alone — no algorithm, no check, no human judgment. Precept finds the one valid state the same way: the structure of the definition makes any other result impossible. Same definition, same data, same outcome: as inevitable as a weight falling.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The plumb line means Precept's outcomes are structurally inevitable because the definition, like gravity, permits only one true result from the same inputs.
candidates.decision: Kept as a distinctive silhouette with a compelling determinism metaphor; conceptual bridge is one step removed from core Precept differentiators.

candidates.title: The vivid solid disc is the complete Precept governed domain — bounded, closed, total. The ruled inspection slot bisecting it is not a flaw but a structural feature: at any state, at any time, nothing inside is hidden. Prevention and inspectability are both built into the form.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The vivid domain circle means Precept is both a closed domain and a fully transparent one because the disc bounds every valid state while the inspection slot makes the interior always visible.
candidates.decision: Kept as a radical departure candidate; bold and visually distinct, but monochrome test is weak and conceptual bridge needs strengthening.

candidates.title: The builder's set square tests whether an angle is exactly 90° — nothing approximate passes. Precept's compiler is the set square: the definition sets the governed standard, and any state that does not fit precisely is structurally impossible, not flagged after the fact. The corner mark is the traditional notation for exact structural soundness.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The set square means Precept's compile-time checking is a structural test because the definition is the precision standard against which all states are measured — approximate fits do not exist.
candidates.decision: Kept for a strong compile-time-checking metaphor; L-shape silhouette needs more distinctiveness to avoid reading as a floor plan corner.

candidates.title: A master die (hexagonal, like a coin die) with a Greek cross struck into its face. Every instance of the entity is struck from the same die — the definition is the master. The cross represents the four governed operations: CreateInstance, Fire, Inspect, Update.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The struck die means every Precept instance is structurally identical because all are struck from the same immutable definition, just as coins from a die bear identical marks.
candidates.decision: Kept as a new silhouette family (hexagonal); conceptual bridge needs strengthening to connect more specifically to Precept's prevention guarantee.

candidates.title: A circular authority seal — like a hallmark on precious metal — pressed once by the compiler and impossible to undo. The disc is the complete, bounded contract. The P as negative space shows the inspectable interior: nothing inside the governed domain is hidden from inspection.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The authority seal means Precept's contract is irrevocable because the definition is pressed once and the seal's structure itself prevents any deviation.
candidates.decision: Kept for the circular form and crimson palette, which are genuinely new; but the P void concept overlaps significantly with 06-negative-p and needs a stronger distinguishing bridge.

candidates.title: Two architectural slabs separated by a single precise aperture show that invalid paths are structurally absent — the material has no other opening, so nothing can pass except through the one governed threshold. The bright centerline is the rule itself.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The threshold means governed access because the contract contains exactly one allowed opening and no accidental alternatives.
candidates.decision: Archived — below promotion threshold (3.5) and superseded by stronger structural metaphors in the shortlisted set.

candidates.title: A solid disc with one precise V-notch shows that the only valid entry is structural — the material itself has no other opening, so invalid states are absent rather than refused. The notch is the one governed path; everything else is matter.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The notched disc means one valid path because the contract is a closed body with only a single structural entry point.
candidates.decision: Archived — below promotion threshold (3.5) and its one-entry-point metaphor is covered more strongly by the governed ring and other candidates.

candidates.title: A gated decision node where a governed path passes (green) and a blocked path is rejected (red dashed) attempts to embody Precept's prevention guarantee. The connection to structural impossibility is present but overly literal — it depicts the mechanism rather than the feeling of governed certainty.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The gated decision means prevention because one branch is allowed and another is explicitly blocked, but it still reads as a workflow diagram.
candidates.decision: Archived as too literal and too dependent on diagram language rather than an ownable emblem.

candidates.title: A source node forking into a governed arc (green) and a structurally absent path (red dashed) shows that some routes cannot exist — echoing Precept's promise that invalid states are impossible, not merely caught. Thin lines and color-dependent meaning limit its impact as a compact mark.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The arc fork means some paths cannot exist, but the composition still explains the product as a flowchart instead of a symbol.
candidates.decision: Archived because the meaning depends on explanatory color and thin-line diagram logic.

candidates.title: Sequential state transitions with stacked nodes attempt to show an ordered, governed set of steps. The design explores visual hierarchy between predecessor, current, and blocked states but reads as a generic workflow sketch rather than an emblem of structural certainty.
candidates.conceptualBridge: The stacked nodes mean ordered state change, but the result reads as a generic process diagram instead of a durable product mark.
candidates.decision: Archived as a useful baseline reference but not a viable brand direction.
